title: Dropbox / File Sync — System Design
description: Design a file storage and sync service: upload files, store them durably and cheaply, and keep the same folder consistent across a user's devices. The themes are splitting files into content-addressed chunks, deduplication, the metadata/block split, and change notification.

nodes.label: Sync Client (Device)
nodes.description: Watches local folders, chunks files, and syncs changes both ways.

nodes.label: API Gateway / LB
nodes.description: Routes and authenticates sync and metadata requests.

nodes.label: Metadata Service
nodes.description: Owns the file/namespace tree, versions, and chunk lists.

nodes.label: Metadata Store
nodes.description: Authoritative file metadata, versions, and chunk manifests, sharded by user/namespace.

nodes.label: Block Service
nodes.description: Stores and retrieves content-addressed chunks; enforces dedup.

nodes.label: Block / Object Store
nodes.description: Durable storage for unique chunks, keyed by content hash.

nodes.label: Chunk Index
nodes.description: hash -> exists? + refcount; powers dedup and garbage collection.

nodes.label: Notification Service
nodes.description: Long-poll/push channel telling other devices a namespace changed.

nodes.label: Change Notification Queue
nodes.description: Buffers change events fanned out to a user's other devices.

nodes.label: Download CDN
nodes.description: Caches popular/public chunks near users for fast download.

nodes.label: Garbage Collector
nodes.description: Reclaims chunks whose refcount drops to zero.

functional: Upload, download, and delete files of arbitrary size.
Sync a folder so the same files appear on all of a user's devices.
Versioning: keep history; restore a previous version.
Share files/folders with other users (read or write).
Resumable uploads that survive flaky connections.
nonFunctional: Durable: never lose a stored file (11 nines style durability).
Storage-efficient: deduplicate identical content to control cost.
Bandwidth-efficient sync: transfer only what changed, not whole files.
Sync latency: a change appears on other devices within seconds.
Scale to billions of files / exabytes; read-heavy on downloads.

capacity.label: Files
capacity.note: Across hundreds of millions of users.

capacity.label: Chunk size
capacity.note: Unit of dedup, transfer, and resumability.

capacity.label: Dedup ratio
capacity.note: Shared files, re-uploads, and unchanged chunks dedup heavily.

api.description: Given chunk hashes, return which are missing so the client only uploads those.
Upload one content-addressed chunk. Idempotent: same hash, same content.
Commit a new file version referencing its ordered chunk hashes. Triggers sync notifications.
Long-poll for changes to the user's namespace since a cursor (the sync delta).

dataModel.name: files (metadata)
dataModel.note: Current state of a path in a namespace.

dataModel.name: versions
dataModel.note: Immutable version history per file for restore.

dataModel.name: chunks
dataModel.note: Content-addressed dedup index (one row per unique chunk).

patterns.name: Content-addressed chunking
patterns.what: Split files into fixed/variable chunks and key each by the hash of its content.
patterns.whenToUse: Storage where identical content recurs and partial transfer matters.

patterns.name: Deduplication
patterns.what: Store each unique chunk once; reference it by hash from many files.
patterns.whenToUse: When the same bytes appear across files, versions, and users.

patterns.name: Metadata/data split
patterns.what: Keep the file tree and chunk manifests in a transactional store; keep bytes in cheap object storage.
patterns.whenToUse: Large-blob systems needing both rich metadata and cheap durable bytes.

patterns.name: Delta sync
patterns.what: Transfer only changed chunks by comparing hashes before upload/download.
patterns.whenToUse: Bandwidth-constrained sync of large files with small edits.

patterns.name: Change notification (long-poll/push)
patterns.what: Tell other devices a namespace changed so they pull the delta.
patterns.whenToUse: Near-real-time multi-device sync without constant polling.

patterns.name: Reference-counted GC
patterns.what: Track chunk refcounts and reclaim chunks no version references.
patterns.whenToUse: Dedup stores where deletes must eventually free space safely.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Upload & Download the Whole File (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. On every change, the client uploads the entire file to the server, which stores it as one blob; to sync, another device downloads the whole file back.

This is correct and trivial — and it exposes the problems the rest of the design exists to solve. A one-byte edit to a 500 MB file re-uploads all 500 MB; the same file shared by ten users is stored ten times; and an upload that drops at 90% must restart from zero because there are no independent pieces to resume from.
So the baseline motivates everything that follows: split files into content-addressed chunks so you transfer and store only what is new, and resume from the last good chunk.
steps.decisionPrompt: A user edits one paragraph of a 500 MB document. If the client re-uploads the whole file, what does that cost in bandwidth, storage, and sync latency at scale?
whyNow: Establishing the whole-file baseline first makes it obvious why files must be chunked and content-addressed — every later step removes a specific pain (re-transfer, duplication, no resume) from here.

recap.after: Files stored and synced as whole blobs.
recap.newRisk: Tiny edits re-transfer the whole file, identical content is stored many times, and a dropped upload restarts from zero — no dedup and no resumability.

traps.why: Bandwidth and storage scale with file size on every change, identical files aren't deduplicated, and interrupted transfers can't resume.
traps.instead: Split files into content-addressed chunks; transfer and store only the chunks that changed, and resume per chunk.

steps.title: 2. Split Files into Content-Addressed Chunks
description: The client splits each file into chunks (~4 MB) and hashes each chunk's content. The hash is the chunk's identity (content-addressed storage).
Chunking is the unit of everything: transfer, dedup, resumability, and versioning. A small edit re-chunks only the affected region, not the whole file.
The client uploads chunks to the block service and records the ordered list of chunk hashes that reconstructs the file.

options.name: Content-defined chunking (rolling hash) (default)
pros: Chunk boundaries follow content, so inserting a byte at the front only re-chunks the first region — the rest keep their hashes.
Edits re-upload a handful of chunks, which is what makes dedup and delta sync actually save bandwidth.
Stable boundaries maximize cross-version and cross-file chunk reuse.
cons: Rolling-hash boundary detection is more CPU on the client than a simple split.
Variable chunk sizes complicate accounting and storage layout slightly.
Pathological content can produce very small or very large chunks unless bounded.

options.name: Fixed-size chunking (e.g. 4 MB blocks)
pros: Trivial to implement: split every N bytes, hash each block.
Uniform chunk sizes make storage accounting and indexing simple.
Cheap on the client — no rolling-hash computation.
cons: Inserting one byte at the front shifts every boundary, so all chunk hashes change and dedup collapses.
A small edit can force re-upload of the whole file, defeating delta sync.
Reuse across versions/files is poor whenever offsets shift.

steps.decisionPrompt: Fixed-size vs content-defined (variable) chunking — what does each cost when you insert a byte at the start of a file?

concepts.definition: A block of bytes identified by the hash of its own content.
concepts.whyItMatters: Identical content has the same id everywhere, which is what makes dedup and delta sync possible.
concepts.example: chunk hash = SHA-256(bytes); same bytes -> same hash -> stored once.

whyNow: Chunking is the foundational decision; dedup, delta sync, and resumability all derive from it.

recap.after: Files are ordered lists of content-addressed chunks.
recap.newRisk: Many chunks will be identical across files/users — storing them all is wasteful; need dedup.

traps.trap: Fixed-size chunking with no rolling hash.
traps.why: Inserting one byte at the front shifts all boundaries, so every chunk hash changes and dedup collapses.
traps.instead: Use content-defined chunking (rolling hash) so boundaries are stable under edits.

steps.title: 3. Deduplicate: Store Each Chunk Once
description: Before uploading, the client asks the block service which chunk hashes already exist (the chunk index). It uploads only the missing ones.
This dedups across versions (an edit re-sends only changed chunks), across files, and across users (the same shared PDF is stored once).
The chunk index maps hash -> exists + refcount; the actual bytes live once in the object store keyed by hash.
steps.decisionPrompt: How much bandwidth does saving a 1 GB video with a 1-second trim re-cost?
whyNow: Dedup is the cost story for a storage system; the 'check-then-upload-missing' handshake is the concrete mechanism interviewers want.

recap.after: Only novel chunks transfer and store.
recap.newRisk: Need a place for the file tree, versions, and chunk manifests — separate from the bytes.

steps.title: 4. Split Metadata from Block Storage
description: Keep two planes. The metadata service owns the namespace tree, file versions, and each file's ordered chunk-hash manifest in a transactional database. The block plane stores opaque bytes cheaply and durably in object storage.
A 'file' is just metadata: a path + version + list of chunk hashes. Reconstructing it means fetching those chunks. This keeps the rich, queryable, transactional part small and the huge part cheap.
Commit metadata last: upload chunks first, then atomically commit the new version referencing them. A crash mid-upload leaves orphan chunks (GC handles them), never a dangling metadata pointer.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why not store file bytes directly in the metadata database?

concepts.definition: Separate the transactional file tree/manifest from the bulk byte storage.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets metadata be consistent and queryable while bytes stay cheap and massively scalable.
concepts.example: Postgres-style metadata DB + S3-style block store.

whyNow: The metadata/data split is the architectural backbone; ordering chunk-upload before metadata-commit is the correctness nuance.

recap.after: Transactional metadata + cheap durable blocks; commit ordering is safe.
recap.newRisk: Multiple devices editing the same namespace need to converge — sync + conflicts.

traps.trap: Committing metadata before chunks are durably stored.
traps.why: A reader could resolve a version whose chunks don't exist yet.
traps.instead: Upload chunks first; commit the version referencing them last.

steps.title: 5. Delta Sync Across Devices
description: Each device tracks a cursor of the last namespace version it has seen. To sync, it asks for the changes since its cursor and gets a delta of changed file metadata.
For each changed file, the device compares the new chunk-hash list against what it already has locally and downloads only the missing chunks — delta sync in both directions.
Downloads come from the block service or a CDN; reconstruct the file from its ordered chunks. Resumable because chunks are independent units.
steps.decisionPrompt: A 500 MB file changed by 4 MB. How much does each other device download?
whyNow: Bandwidth-efficient sync is a headline requirement; reusing the hash-compare from dedup on the download side is the elegant part.

recap.after: Devices transfer only changed chunks, resumably, via CDN.
recap.newRisk: How do devices learn there's a change without constant polling? And what if two devices edit at once?

flows.name: Delta sync: pull changes -> download only missing chunks
flows.note: A device pulls the namespace delta since its cursor, then downloads only the chunks it lacks (via CDN), reconstructing the file locally.

steps.title: 6. Change Notification and Conflict Handling
description: On commit, the metadata service publishes a 'namespace changed' event; a notification service (long-poll or push) tells the user's other devices to pull the delta — near-real-time without polling.
Concurrent edits are detected with optimistic versioning: a commit carries the baseVersion it edited; if the server has advanced past it, the commit conflicts.
Resolve conflicts safely by keeping both: create a 'conflicted copy' rather than silently overwriting, and let the user reconcile. Never lose a write.

options.name: Conflicted copy (keep both) (default)
pros: Never loses a write: the losing edit is preserved as a 'conflicted copy' for the user to reconcile.
Detection is cheap — an optimistic baseVersion check on commit, no locking.
Honest about ambiguity: surfaces the conflict to the human who can actually resolve it.
cons: Users must manually merge the duplicate files, which is annoying.
Conflicted copies proliferate for files edited on many devices at once.
Doesn't merge content — two near-identical files, not one reconciled file.

options.name: Last-writer-wins overwrite
pros: Simplest possible policy: the most recent commit wins, no extra files.
Devices always converge to a single version with no user action.
No conflicted-copy clutter to manage.
cons: Silently destroys the losing device's edits — unacceptable for a file sync product.
'Most recent' depends on clocks/arrival order, which can be wrong under skew.
User has no way to recover the overwritten work without version history.

options.name: Automatic content merge (OT/CRDT)
pros: Merges concurrent edits into one file automatically — no duplicate copies for the user.
Great experience for collaborative document types where edits compose.
Devices converge to a single reconciled state without manual work.
cons: Only works for structured/text content the merge logic understands — opaque binaries (video, zip) can't be merged.
OT/CRDT machinery is complex and adds per-document metadata and ordering state.
Wrong for a general blob store; most files here are not collaboratively edited text.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two devices edit the same file offline, then both reconnect. What should happen?

concepts.definition: A commit names the version it was based on; the server rejects it if state moved on.
concepts.whyItMatters: Detects concurrent edits without locking the file.
concepts.example: Commit baseVersion=7 fails if the file is now at version 8 -> conflict.

whyNow: Notification turns 'eventually correct' into 'syncs in seconds'; the conflict policy is the data-safety nuance senior interviewers probe.

recap.before: Devices polled and could clobber edits.
recap.after: Push-driven sync; concurrent edits kept as conflicted copies.
recap.newRisk: Deletes and old versions accumulate dead chunks — need GC.

traps.instead: Keep both via a conflicted copy and let the user reconcile.

steps.title: 7. Garbage Collection and Durability
description: Dedup means a chunk may be referenced by many files/versions. Deleting a file or pruning old versions doesn't free a chunk until nothing references it — track a refcount per chunk.

A background garbage collector finds chunks whose refcount hit zero and deletes them from the block store. Do this lazily and safely (grace period / mark-and-sweep) to avoid deleting a chunk a concurrent upload just referenced.
Durability comes from the object store's replication/erasure coding across zones; metadata is replicated and backed up. Orphan chunks from interrupted uploads are reclaimed the same way.

options.name: Refcount + grace-period sweep (default)
pros: A per-chunk refcount makes 'is this chunk still needed?' an O(1) lookup.
A grace period before deleting a zero-ref chunk avoids racing a concurrent upload that just referenced it.
Lazy background sweep keeps deletion off the hot upload/download path.
cons: Refcounts can drift (crashes mid-update) and need periodic auditing against metadata.
Grace period delays reclaiming space, so freed bytes linger.
Maintaining accurate refcounts across every commit/delete is bookkeeping-heavy.

options.name: Immediate reference counting (delete at zero)
pros: Reclaims space instantly when the last reference drops — no lingering garbage.
Simple mental model: delete the chunk the moment its count hits zero.
No periodic full scan of the chunk index needed.
cons: Races a just-started upload that is about to reference the chunk — can delete live data.
A lost refcount decrement leaks a chunk forever; a lost increment risks premature deletion.
Synchronous deletes on the commit path add latency and contention.

options.name: Mark-and-sweep tracing (no refcounts)
pros: No refcounts to maintain or drift: walk all live file manifests, mark reachable chunks, sweep the rest.
Self-correcting — each run recomputes truth from metadata, so leaks and orphans heal.
Robust to crashes during commits since reachability is derived, not incrementally tracked.
cons: A full mark phase scans all metadata and chunk ids — expensive at trillions of chunks.
Must snapshot or coordinate with in-flight uploads so live chunks aren't swept.
Reclamation only happens per sweep cycle, not continuously.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you avoid GC deleting a chunk that a just-started upload is about to reference?

concepts.definition: Reclaim a shared resource only when its reference count reaches zero.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets dedup and deletes coexist without freeing in-use data.
concepts.example: Delete file -> decrement its chunks' refcounts -> GC removes any that hit 0.

whyNow: Closing on GC + durability shows the dedup design is complete: space is actually reclaimed, and data is safe.

recap.after: Refcounted GC reclaims unused chunks; bytes durably replicated.
recap.newRisk: GC races and zone failures — bounded by grace periods, mark-and-sweep, and cross-zone replication.

failureDrills.scenario: An upload references a chunk just as GC is about to delete it.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: GC respects a grace period / re-checks refcount before delete, so the live chunk survives.
failureDrills.mitigation: Mark-and-sweep with a delay; increment refcount before commit makes a chunk reachable.

failureDrills.scenario: An availability zone holding chunks fails.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads/writes continue from replicas in other zones.
failureDrills.mitigation: Cross-zone replication / erasure coding in the object store.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Chunked, Deduplicated File Sync
finalDesign.description: Clients chunk files into content-addressed blocks, check the chunk index, and upload only missing chunks to the block store; then they commit a new file version (ordered chunk hashes) to the transactional metadata service. The metadata/block split keeps structure consistent and bytes cheap. The notification service pushes change events so other devices pull deltas and download only changed chunks (via CDN). Concurrent edits become conflicted copies; reference-counted GC reclaims unused chunks; the object store provides cross-zone durability.

functional.how: Chunked upload to block store; metadata commit; refcount on delete.
Cursor-based delta + push notification; download only changed chunks.
Immutable version rows referencing chunk manifests.
Independent content-addressed chunks; re-check missing and resume.

nonFunctional.how: Cross-zone replication/erasure coding in the object store; replicated metadata.
Content-addressed dedup stores each chunk once.
Hash-compare transfers only changed chunks both ways.
Push notifications drive pulls instead of polling.

say: Confirm sync (multi-device) and sharing, not just blob storage.
Pin durability, dedup, and bandwidth-efficient sync as the hard goals.
Agree sync within seconds is acceptable (eventual, not instant).
Delta sync with cursors + hash compare.
Change notification (long-poll/push) and conflict handling (conflicted copies).
Tradeoffs: chunking scheme, commit ordering, GC safety, conflict policy.
Extensions: sharing/ACLs, CDN tuning, client-side encryption.

expectations: May miss dedup handshake and conflicts.
Designs content-addressed dedup and delta sync.
Reasons about content-defined chunking vs fixed.
Designs safe reference-counted GC with races in mind.
Discusses durability (erasure coding), sharing/ACLs, and encryption tradeoffs.

followUps: Fixed-size vs content-defined chunking — when does each win?
How do you safely GC chunks without deleting one an in-flight upload needs?
How would you implement sharing and per-folder ACLs?
How do you support client-side (zero-knowledge) encryption while keeping dedup?
How would you handle a single huge file (100 GB) and its sync efficiently?
